tier_2_handbook.definition: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) is a malignant epithelial neoplasm arising from the glandular mucosa of the small intestine, encompassing the duodenum (derived from the foregut/midgut), jejunum, and ileum (both midgut). Anatomically, the small bowel extends from the pylorus to the ileocecal valve. SBA accounts for an estimated 30% to 40% of all small intestinal cancer diagnoses, with neuroendocrine tumors, gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs), and lymphomas comprising the other major histologies [4]. The clinical definition is based on pathologic confirmation of adenocarcinoma in one of these segments, with staging according to the AJCC 8th edition TNM system for small intestine adenocarcinoma [6]. SBA is a rare malignancy: in 2024, an estimated 12,440 new cases of small bowel cancer were projected in the United States, with approximately 2090 deaths [1]. The disease is often diagnosed at an advanced stage due to nonspecific presenting symptoms such as gastric outlet obstruction (duodenal tumors), cramping abdominal pain (jejunal/ileal tumors), or occult gastrointestinal bleeding [36,41,42]. For duodenal primaries, discrimination from ampullary, distal common bile duct, or pancreatic head malignancies is required; the epicenter of the tumor or precursor lesion should not be at the ampulla, and >75% of the mass should not be within the ampulla [SBA-B]. Regional lymph nodes differ by primary site: for duodenum, nodes include retropancreatic, hepatic artery, inferior pancreaticoduodenal, and superior mesenteric; for jejunum/ileum, nodes include cecal (terminal ileum only), ileocolic (terminal ileum only), superior mesenteric, mesenteric, and NOS [SBA-B]. The NCCN Guidelines define distinct clinical pathways for duodenal versus jejunal/ileal primaries and for mismatch repair proficient (pMMR)/microsatellite stable (MSS) versus deficient MMR (dMMR)/MSI-high (MSI-H) or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated phenotypes [SBA-2 through SBA-5].

subtypes.name: Duodenal Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Adenocarcinoma arising in the duodenum, the most common subsite, accounting for approximately 52% to 58% of all SBA cases [36-39]. These tumors often present with gastric outlet obstruction or jaundice and require careful distinction from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries via endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) [SBA-A]. Surgical management typically involves pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) for tumors in the second portion or invading the ampulla/pancreas; limited segmentectomy may be considered for anti-mesenteric lesions in the third/fourth segments [SBA-C].
subtypes.key_associations: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) – duodenum/periampullary region is most common SBA location in FAP [20]; Lynch syndrome; Crohn's disease; celiac disease

subtypes.name: Jejunal Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Adenocarcinoma arising in the jejunum, the second most common subsite. These tumors often present with cramping abdominal pain or obstruction. Surgical management is segmental resection with margins of at least 5–10 cm and lymph node dissection to the origin of feeder vessels [SBA-C]. Radiation therapy is not generally indicated for jejunal lesions [SBA-E].
subtypes.key_associations: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (PJS) – hamartomatous polyps predominantly in jejunum and ileum [20,21]; Crohn's disease

subtypes.name: Ileal Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Adenocarcinoma arising in the ileum, the least common subsite. Distal ileal tumors are managed with segmentectomy plus right hemicolectomy [SBA-C]. Peritoneal carcinomatosis is more frequent from jejunal/ileal primaries than from duodenal primaries [108]. Surveillance screening is especially important in patients with Crohn's disease due to elevated risk [SBA-F].
subtypes.key_associations: Crohn's disease – distal SBA risk increased (RR 3.70) [11,12]; Lynch syndrome

subtypes.name: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 Ultra-Hypermutated Phenotype
subtypes.description: A molecular subtype defined by deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) resulting in microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or by polymerase epsilon/delta 1 (POLE/POLD1) mutations conferring an ultra-hypermutated phenotype (e.g., tumor mutational burden >50 mutations/Mb). This subtype is enriched in SBA compared to colorectal cancer, with dMMR occurring in a higher percentage of SBA [34,35]. It has distinct prognostic and therapeutic implications: patients may have improved survival in stage II (extrapolated from CRC data) and are candidates for immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy across all lines of treatment [SBA-D 2; SBA-D 3].
subtypes.frequency: Incidence of dMMR/MSI-H is higher than in CRC; POLE/POLD1 mutation incidence ~0.3% [35]; TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) in 9-11% of SBA [35,155]
subtypes.key_associations: Lynch syndrome (germline MMR gene mutations); potential for excellent response to PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors; may be prognostic for favorable stage II outcomes

subtypes.name: pMMR/MSS (Proficient MMR / Microsatellite Stable)
subtypes.description: The majority of SBA cases, characterized by intact mismatch repair function and microsatellite stability. This subtype does not derive the same degree of benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors as dMMR/MSI-H tumors, although pembrolizumab is a category 2A option for TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) pMMR/MSS tumors [SBA-D 1]. Management follows standard chemotherapy-based approaches.
subtypes.key_associations: KRAS, TP53, APC mutations; less common APC mutations (27%) compared to CRC (76%) [35]; distinct molecular pathogenesis from CRC

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) has a distinct genomic landscape that sets it apart from colorectal cancer (CRC) and gastroesophageal cancers. Comprehensive genomic profiling studies have identified key driver mutations and pathways. KRAS and TP53 alterations are frequently identified in both SBA and CRC, but APC mutations are significantly less common in SBA (27%) compared to CRC (76%, P<.001), suggesting a unique mechanism of neoplastic transformation [35]. SMAD4 and CDKN2A mutations are more commonly observed in SBA than in CRC or gastroesophageal cancers [35]. BRAF mutations occur at a similar rate (~9.1% of SBA), but only about 10% of BRAF-mutant SBA harbor the V600E alteration, compared with >70% in BRAF-mutant CRC [35]. Human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2/ERBB2) alterations are enriched in SBA, with overexpression/amplification providing a therapeutic target [35,122]. Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H)/deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) is more prevalent in SBA than in CRC, with rates of TMB-high (>10 mutations/Mb) in approximately 9% to 11% of SBA versus 4.3% in CRC (P=.001) [35,155]. Programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression is also enhanced in SBA, particularly in those associated with Crohn's disease or celiac disease [120,123]. POLE and POLD1 exonuclease domain mutations, though rare (incidence ~0.3% in both SBA and CRC), confer an ultra-hypermutated phenotype (often TMB>50 mut/Mb) and predict response to immune checkpoint inhibitors [35,152-154]. The frequency of RET and NTRK1/2/3 gene fusions is low but actionable, and testing via multigene panel testing (MGPT) is recommended [SBA-8]. WNT pathway alterations (CTNNB1, RNF43) and cell cycle regulators (CDKN2A, CCND1) are also implicated. The CpG island methylator phenotype (CIMP) may be present in a subset. The genomic profile underscores that SBA is a molecularly distinct entity from CRC, with implications for targeted therapy and immunotherapy selection [35].

epidemiology.incidence: In the United States, the incidence of small bowel adenocarcinoma is approximately 2.6 per 100,000 for males and 2.0 per 100,000 for females [2]. An estimated 12,440 new cases of small bowel cancer (all histologies) occurred in 2024, with approximately 30–40% being adenocarcinoma [1,4]. Incidence is increasing, with an annual percent change of 1.8% between 2006 and 2015, contrasting with decreasing trends for esophageal, gastric, colon, and rectal cancers over the same period [2]. The median age at diagnosis is 66 years [2].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 2090 deaths from small bowel cancer occurred in 2024 [1]. Five-year relative survival for localized SBA is approximately 85%, but drops to only 42% for stage IV (distant metastatic) disease [105]. Stage III/IV SBA has significantly worse outcomes compared to earlier stages [6,58,59].
epidemiology.trends: Incidence has been rising (1.8% annual increase from 2006 to 2015) [2]. This trend may be partly due to improved detection, but a true increase is suspected. SBA is more often diagnosed at advanced stages than CRC, highlighting diagnostic challenges [MS-19].
epidemiology.demographics: Males and females are affected relatively equally (incidence 2.6 vs 2.0 per 100,000) [2]. The majority of cases arise in the duodenum (52–58%), followed by jejunum (15–29%), ileum (10–13%), and unspecified location (4–16%) [36-39]. Patients with SBA tend to be younger at diagnosis and present with higher stage and grade compared to those with CRC [40]. No specific race/ethnicity data were provided in the source beyond general U.S. population statistics.

risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in MMR genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Lifetime risk of SBA is 4%, representing a relative risk >100 compared to the general population [27,28]. Universal MMR/MSI testing is recommended for all SBA patients to identify Lynch syndrome [SBA-B].

Autosomal dominant germline mutation in APC gene (or MUTYH in attenuated FAP). Lifetime risk of SBA is estimated at 3% to 5%, with duodenum and periampullary region being the most common locations [18-20].

Autosomal dominant condition, majority due to STK11 (LKB1) mutations. Relative risk of SBA is 520 compared to general population; lifetime risk estimated between 1.7% and 13% [20,25,26]. Hamartomatous polyps predominantly in jejunum and ileum [20,21].
risk_factors.strength: Very strong; RR 520, lifetime risk up to 13%

risk_factors.detail: Chronic inflammation of the small bowel. A retrospective multicenter cohort study of 9100 IBD patients found relative risk of small bowel cancer 3.70 (95% CI, 1.23–11.13) [11]. Distal SBA is more common. Rate of death and cancer remission did not differ between patients who maintained IBD treatment versus those who stopped [11].

In a cohort study of 48,119 individuals with celiac disease, 0.06% (n=29) were diagnosed with SBA within 1 year of celiac diagnosis, suggesting a possible link [17]. Association is poorly understood and distinct from CRC (celiac disease is not a risk factor for CRC) [MS-3].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; incidence 0.06% within 1 year of celiac diagnosis

risk_factors.detail: A systematic review reported high alcohol consumption may increase SBA risk [9]. A pooled analysis of over 500,000 individuals in the Asia Cohort Consortium showed a non-significant trend toward increased risk with high alcohol intake [10].
risk_factors.strength: Weak to moderate; systematic review suggests increased risk, pooled analysis shows non-significant trend

risk_factors.detail: Systematic review reported smoking may increase SBA risk [9]. However, the pooled Asia Cohort Consortium analysis did not identify smoking as a risk factor [10].
risk_factors.strength: Conflicting evidence; systematic review positive, large pooled analysis negative

risk_factors.factor: Dietary factors (low fiber, high red/processed meat, high sugary drinks)
risk_factors.detail: Systematic review reported that low intake of fiber and high intake of red/processed meat and sugary drinks may increase the risk of SBA [9].
risk_factors.strength: Weak; based on systematic review of limited data

risk_factors.detail: Pooled analysis of Asia Cohort Consortium reported elevated BMI was associated with a non-significant trend towards increased SBA risk [10].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) presents with nonspecific symptoms that often delay diagnosis. The most common presentations are gastric outlet obstruction (for duodenal tumors), cramping abdominal pain (for jejunal/ileal tumors), and occult gastrointestinal bleeding, which occurs in approximately one-quarter to one-third of cases [MS-5]. Duodenal tumors account for 52%–58% of cases, jejunal 15%–29%, and ileal 10%–13% [MS-5]. Patients tend to be younger at diagnosis and present at a higher stage and grade compared to colorectal cancer [MS-5]. Many patients present emergently with malignant small bowel obstruction, accounting for 22%–57.9% of cases [MS-9]. Other features include weight loss, nausea, vomiting, and anemia. Due to the rarity of SBA and overlapping symptoms with benign conditions, the diagnosis is often made at an advanced stage.

symptoms.name: Gastric outlet obstruction
symptoms.detail: Most common in duodenal SBA, presenting with postprandial vomiting, early satiety, and epigastric fullness

symptoms.name: Cramping abdominal pain
symptoms.detail: Most common in jejunal or ileal SBA, often colicky and intermittent due to partial obstruction

symptoms.name: Occult gastrointestinal bleeding
symptoms.detail: Presents as iron-deficiency anemia, melena, or hematochezia

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unintentional weight loss due to obstruction and malignancy

signs.name: Abdominal distension
signs.detail: Due to obstruction or peritoneal metastases

signs.name: Palpable abdominal mass
signs.detail: May be felt in advanced disease

signs.name: Jaundice
signs.detail: With duodenal tumors causing biliary obstruction

atypical_or_missed: Symptoms often mimic irritable bowel syndrome or other benign conditions, leading to delayed diagnosis
Patients may be asymptomatic until advanced stage
Acute abdomen due to perforation can be first presentation
In patients with Crohn's disease, symptoms may be attributed to inflammatory flare rather than malignancy

red_flags: Gastric outlet obstruction or small bowel obstruction requiring urgent intervention [SBA-2, SBA-4]
Unexplained iron-deficiency anemia due to occult gastrointestinal bleeding [MS-5]
Palpable abdominal mass or signs of peritoneal carcinomatosis
Jaundice in the setting of duodenal tumor
Emergency presentation with perforation or severe hemorrhage
Worsening abdominal pain in a patient with Crohn's disease or celiac disease [SBA-F]
Positive fecal occult blood with negative upper and lower endoscopy (suggests small bowel source)

diagnostic.rationale: Primary method for detection and pathologic sampling of duodenal lesions; allows direct visualization and tissue acquisition [SBA-A 2 of 2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Detection of duodenal mass, ulceration, or stricture; tissue for histologic confirmation

diagnostic.test: Push- or device-assisted enteroscopy (double- or single-balloon enteroscopy)
diagnostic.rationale: May be used for diagnosis and biopsy of jejunal or ileal lesions, especially when other methods fail; also used for stricture evaluation [SBA-A 2 of 2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Visualization and biopsy of lesions beyond duodenum

diagnostic.rationale: Consider when radiographic and other endoscopic methods fail to identify a suspected primary lesion; not preferred for initial workup due to inability to obtain tissue [SBA-A 2 of 2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Detection of mucosal abnormalities; contraindicated in obstruction or stricture

diagnostic.rationale: May improve visualization of small bowel tumors when conventional CT or MR with contrast is insufficient [SBA-A 1 of 2]
diagnostic.key_findings: Improved sensitivity for intraluminal and mural lesions

diagnostic.test: Complete blood count (CBC), chemistry profile, CA 19-9, CEA
diagnostic.rationale: Baseline assessment of anemia, liver and renal function, and tumor markers for monitoring [SBA-1]

staging.test: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with intravenous and oral contrast
staging.rationale: Primary imaging for staging; evaluates local tumor extent and distant metastases to lung, liver, peritoneum, and nodes [SBA-A 1 of 2]
staging.indication: All patients with SBA for initial staging and resectability assessment

staging.rationale: May be done without contrast unless combined with abdominal CT [SBA-A 1 of 2]

staging.test: MRI of abdomen/pelvis with IV gadolinium
staging.rationale: Alternative when IV iodinated contrast is contraindicated; useful for characterization of indeterminate liver lesions [SBA-A 1 of 2]
staging.indication: Contraindication to CT contrast, or need for better liver lesion characterization

staging.rationale: Used in initial workup of suspected duodenal malignancies, particularly with biliary obstruction, to distinguish primary site [SBA-A 1 of 2]
staging.indication: Biliary obstruction or uncertainty about tumor origin

staging.rationale: Enhanced pre-therapeutic staging of proximal small bowel malignancies; discerns duodenal from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries [SBA-1, SBA-A 2 of 2]
staging.indication: Duodenal tumors (especially when needed for clinical staging)

staging.rationale: Not indicated for routine staging; may be considered when CT or MR results are equivocal, including evaluation of potential peritoneal disease [SBA-A 1 of 2]

Not routinely indicated for clearly resectable early-stage disease; may be considered when occult peritoneal or metastatic disease is suspected (locally advanced tumors, high-risk histology, elevated tumor markers, discordant findings) and when results would alter management [SBA-C]

biomarkers.rationale: Universal testing recommended for all newly diagnosed SBA; important for prognosis, identification of Lynch syndrome, and treatment selection (especially checkpoint inhibitor therapy) [SBA-B]
Required for metastatic disease to guide targeted therapy; RAS status predicts lack of benefit from EGFR inhibitors [SBA-8, SBA-D 1 of 12]
Identifies patients who may benefit from HER2-directed therapies (e.g., trastuzumab, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan) [SBA-D 1 of 12]
biomarkers.when: Metastatic disease; also consider for earlier stages

biomarkers.rationale: TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) identifies patients who may respond to pembrolizumab (category 2B) [SBA-D 1 of 12, SBA-D 3 of 12]
biomarkers.when: Metastatic disease, especially if considering checkpoint inhibitors

biomarkers.rationale: Associated with ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB>50 mut/Mb) and potential benefit from checkpoint inhibitors [SBA-3, SBA-5, SBA-D 2 of 12]
biomarkers.when: Metastatic disease; part of multigene panel testing

biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients who may benefit from tropomyosin receptor kinase (TRK) inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib) [SBA-D 1 of 12]
biomarkers.when: Metastatic disease with no satisfactory alternative treatment

biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients who may benefit from selpercatinib [SBA-D 1 of 12]

do_not_miss: Universal MMR or MSI testing for all newly diagnosed SBA – critical for Lynch syndrome detection and immunotherapy eligibility [SBA-B]
Multigene panel testing (MGPT) for metastatic disease to capture all actionable mutations (RAS, BRAF, HER2, NTRK, RET, POLE/POLD1, TMB) [SBA-8]
EUS for duodenal tumors to distinguish from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries [SBA-1]
Assessment for celiac disease and Crohn's disease in appropriate clinical contexts [SBA-B]
Genetic counseling for all patients – SBA has strong association with Lynch syndrome, FAP, PJS [SBA-1]
avoid: Routine FDG-PET/CT as efficacy compared to CT or MR has not been established [SBA-A 1 of 2]
Routine capsule endoscopy (cannot obtain tissue; contraindicated in obstruction) [SBA-A 2 of 2]
Routine diagnostic laparoscopy for clearly resectable early-stage disease [SBA-C]
Use of cetuximab or panitumumab for RAS wild-type SBA – no clinically meaningful activity [MS-12]
Use of trifluridine-tipiracil or regorafenib – no data to support use in SBA [MS-12]

staging.system: AJCC 8th edition (2017) TNM staging for small intestine adenocarcinoma [ST-1]

t_stages.description: Tumor invades the lamina propria or submucosa
Tumor invades through the muscularis propria into the subserosa, or extends into nonperitonealized perimuscular tissue (mesentery or retroperitoneum) without serosal penetration

Tumor perforates the visceral peritoneum or directly invades other organs or structures (e.g., other loops of small intestine, mesentery of adjacent loops of bowel, abdominal wall by way of serosa; for duodenum only, invasion of pancreas or bile duct)

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed
Metastasis in one or two regional lymph nodes
Metastasis in three or more regional lymph nodes

stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Data not specifically reported for stage 0

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor confined to lamina propria/submucosa (T1) or muscularis propria (T2), node-negative
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Localized disease (stage I–III combined) 5-year relative survival ≈ 85% [MS-10]

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor extends through muscularis propria into subserosa or nonperitonealized perimuscular tissue, node-negative
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (resection; adjuvant therapy considered for high-risk features)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor perforates visceral peritoneum or directly invades other organs/structures, node-negative; high-risk stage II
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (resection; recommended adjuvant therapy for T4N0)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with 1–2 positive regional lymph nodes
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Localized disease (stage I–III combined) 5-year relative survival ≈ 85% [MS-10]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (resection and adjuvant chemotherapy; consider chemoradiation for margin-positive duodenal cancer)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with ≥3 positive regional lymph nodes; higher nodal burden
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Localized disease (stage I–III combined) 5-year relative survival ≈ 85% [MS-10]
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative (resection and adjuvant chemotherapy; consider chemoradiation for margin-positive duodenal cancer)

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Distant metastatic disease (peritoneal cavity, liver, and other sites)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative (systemic therapy, palliative diversion/stenting; rare metastasectomy in select patients)

staging_pearls: T4 for duodenum includes invasion of pancreas or bile duct, which is not specified for other small bowel sites [ST-1]

Regional lymph node groups differ by primary site: duodenum – retropancreatic, hepatic artery, inferior pancreaticoduodenal, superior mesenteric; jejunum/ileum – cecal/ileocolic (terminal ileum), superior mesenteric, mesenteric NOS [SBA-B]

AJCC recommends minimum evaluation of 8 lymph nodes; NCCN recommends goal of at least 8 lymph nodes for adequate staging, but the College of American Pathologists notes no clear minimum has been established [SBA-B, SBA-C]

For stage II disease, high-risk features include T4, close/positive margins, <5 lymph nodes (duodenal) or <8 (jejunal/ileal), tumor perforation, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, poor differentiation [SBA-6]
Stage III and IV SBA have significantly worse outcomes compared to earlier stages [MS-6]
MSI-H/dMMR status may confer better prognosis in stage II, but this is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data [SBA-B]

management_principles.overview: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) is a rare malignancy with rising incidence, accounting for approximately 3% of gastrointestinal cancers. Treatment philosophy emphasizes a multimodal approach tailored to anatomic subsite (duodenum vs. jejunum/ileum) and molecular profile (pMMR/MSS vs. dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated phenotype). Due to the scarcity of prospective data, many recommendations are extrapolated from colorectal cancer (CRC) studies, and enrollment in clinical trials is strongly encouraged for all stages[MS-1], [MS-2]. The NCCN Panel classifies all recommendations as category 2A unless otherwise noted. Management decisions are guided by resectability, performance status, and biomarker status, with curative-intent surgery for localized disease, neoadjuvant or induction therapy for borderline/unresectable presentations, and palliative systemic therapy for metastatic disease. A multidisciplinary team (MDT) evaluation at a high-volume center is essential, particularly when considering radiation therapy or complex surgical resections like pancreaticoduodenectomy.

by_intent.patient_group: Resectable localized SBA (stage I–III), including T1–2N0, T3N0 (with or without high-risk features), T4N0, and T Any N1–2
by_intent.approach: Surgical resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes (goal ≥8 nodes)[SBA-C]. For duodenal pMMR/MSS tumors, resection alone; for dMMR/MSI-H tumors, neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy may be considered for T4 or bulky primaries[SBA-3], [SBA-5]. Adjuvant therapy is offered based on stage and molecular status: observation for stage I; for stage II pMMR/MSS, observation or chemotherapy (FOLFOX or CAPEOX for high-risk features); for stage III pMMR/MSS, FOLFOX or CAPEOX (3–6 months)[SBA-6]. For dMMR/MSI-H stage II or III, options include FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab or chemotherapy alone[SBA-7].

by_intent.patient_group: Locally unresectable or medically inoperable SBA
by_intent.approach: For pMMR/MSS: induction chemotherapy with FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRINOX, or chemo/RT (duodenal only); for dMMR/MSI-H: checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy as preferred option[SBA-2], [SBA-3], [SBA-4], [SBA-5]. Patients are reevaluated for conversion to resectable disease; if converted, proceed to surgery. For duodenal cancers that remain unresectable after induction chemotherapy, preoperative chemo/RT may be considered[SBA-E].

by_intent.patient_group: Distant metastatic SBA (stage IV) or unresectable recurrence
by_intent.approach: Palliative diversion or stenting for obstruction; systemic therapy based on MMR/MSI status and biomarkers. For pMMR/MSS: FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, or FOLFIRINOX ± bevacizumab (intensive therapy), or fluoropyrimidine monotherapy ± bevacizumab (non-intensive). For dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1: checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (PD-1, PD-L1, or CTLA-4 inhibitors) as any-line therapy[SBA-D 1-2]. Second-line and subsequent therapy includes taxane-based chemotherapy and biomarker-directed agents (e.g., for BRAF V600E, HER2, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C)[SBA-D 1].

History and physical examination every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 5 years; CEA and CA 19-9 every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 5 years; chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 6–12 months for 2 years, then every 12 months for years 3–5. FDG-PET/CT and routine capsule endoscopy are not indicated[SBA-6], [SBA-7]. Surveillance intervals are extrapolated from CRC data[SBA-6 footnote p].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: A multidisciplinary team including surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology, gastroenterology, and genetic counseling is essential for treatment planning. For duodenal primaries, high-volume centers are recommended when RT or complex surgery is considered[SBA-E]. Participation in clinical trials is particularly important given the rarity of SBA and limited data[MS-1].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) guides intensity of therapy. For advanced/metastatic disease, patients with good PS (ECOG 0–2) are candidates for intensive combination regimens (FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRINOX ± bevacizumab). For older adults or those with poor PS, geriatric assessment is recommended to predict treatment risks[SBA-D 3 footnote h]. Non-intensive options (fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine ± bevacizumab) are appropriate for patients who are not candidates for intensive therapy. Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is suitable for dMMR/MSI-H patients regardless of PS, provided no contraindications.

options.label: Resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes
options.recommendation: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) for most duodenal primaries, especially those in second portion or invading ampulla/pancreas; pylorus-preserving variant acceptable if no hereditary condition. Limited segmentectomy may be considered for select cases (anti-mesenteric side, third/fourth segments, or first portion lesions <2 cm)[SBA-C]. Goal: ≥8 lymph nodes assessed[SBA-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: See adjuvant pathways per pathologic stage and MMR status (SBA-6 for pMMR/MSS).

management_pathways.title: Duodenum pMMR/MSS – Locally Unresectable or Medically Inoperable

nodes.criteria: No distant metastases; primary tumor unresectable due to local invasion or medical contraindication to surgery

options.label: Palliative diversion or stent (if obstructed) and/or initial systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Options: FOLFOX or CAPEOX; Fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine; FOLFIRINOX; or Chemo/RT with capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil (for duodenal primaries)[SBA-2].

options.label: Re-evaluate for conversion to resectable disease
options.recommendation: If converted, proceed to surgery; otherwise continue systemic therapy. Preoperative chemo/RT should be considered for patients who remain unresectable after a course of induction chemotherapy[SBA-2 footnote i], [SBA-E].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not applicable; if surgical conversion occurs, adjuvant therapy per pathologic stage.

options.label: Palliative diversion or stent if obstructed
options.recommendation: For symptomatic obstruction.

options.label: Proceed to Metastatic Adenocarcinoma workup and systemic therapy (SBA-8)
options.recommendation: Complete biomarker testing: RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification; MMR or MSI status; multigene panel testing for POLE/POLD1, RET, NTRK 1/2/3, TMB[SBA-8]. See systemic therapy pathways for advanced/metastatic disease (SBA-D).

management_pathways.title: Duodenum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Resectable Primary
branching_basis: MMR/MSI status: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated (e.g., TMB>50 mut/Mb)

options.label: Resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes
options.recommendation: Same surgical principles as pMMR/MSS.

options.label: Consider neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (for T4 or bulky primary)
options.recommendation: See checkpoint inhibitor options in SBA-D 2 of 12[SBA-3 footnote j].

management_pathways.title: Duodenum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Locally Unresectable or Medically Inoperable

nodes.criteria: No distant metastases; primary tumor unresectable

options.label: Palliative diversion or stent (if obstructed) and/or checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy
options.recommendation: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy as first-line (PD-1, PD-L1, or CTLA-4 inhibitors listed in SBA-D 2 of 12)[SBA-3].

options.label: Alternative systemic therapy if checkpoint inhibitor not appropriate
options.recommendation: FOLFOX or CAPEOX, or FOLFIRINOX, or chemo/RT (duodenal only)[SBA-3].

options.label: Re-evaluate for conversion to resectable disease
options.recommendation: If converted, proceed to surgery; otherwise continue systemic therapy.

management_pathways.title: Duodenum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Distant Metastatic Disease

options.label: Palliative diversion or stent if obstructed
Proceed to Metastatic Adenocarcinoma workup and systemic therapy (SBA-8)
options.recommendation: Biomarker testing; see systemic therapy pathways. Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is an option for any line of therapy[SBA-D 2].

options.label: Resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes
options.recommendation: Segmentectomy with lymph node dissection down to origin of feeder vessels; margins of at least 5–10 cm on each side of tumor. For distal ileal tumors, terminal ileal resection with right hemicolectomy[SBA-C]. Goal: ≥8 lymph nodes assessed.

adjuvant_logic.summary: See adjuvant pathways per pathologic stage (SBA-6).

management_pathways.title: Jejunum/Ileum pMMR/MSS – Locally Unresectable or Medically Inoperable

nodes.criteria: No distant metastases; primary tumor unresectable

options.label: Palliative diversion or bypass (if obstructed) and/or systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Options: FOLFOX or CAPEOX; Fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine; FOLFIRINOX[SBA-4]. Note: RT is not generally indicated for jejunum/ileum[SBA-E].

options.label: Re-evaluate for conversion to resectable disease
options.recommendation: If converted, proceed to surgery; otherwise continue systemic therapy.

options.label: Palliative diversion (if obstructed)
Proceed to Metastatic Adenocarcinoma workup and systemic therapy (SBA-8)
options.recommendation: Biomarker testing; see systemic therapy pathways.

management_pathways.title: Jejunum/Ileum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Resectable Primary

options.label: Resection with en bloc removal of regional lymph nodes
options.recommendation: Segmental resection as per pMMR/MSS.

options.label: Consider neoadjuvant checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy (for T4 or bulky primary)
options.recommendation: See SBA-D 2 of 12[SBA-5 footnote j].

management_pathways.title: Jejunum/Ileum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Locally Unresectable or Medically Inoperable

nodes.criteria: No distant metastases; primary tumor unresectable

options.label: Palliative diversion or bypass (if obstructed) and/or checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy
options.recommendation: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy as first-line (see SBA-D 2)[SBA-5].

options.label: Alternative systemic therapy
options.recommendation: FOLFOX or CAPEOX, or FOLFIRINOX[SBA-5].

options.label: Re-evaluate for conversion to resectable disease
options.recommendation: If converted, proceed to surgery; otherwise continue systemic therapy.

management_pathways.title: Jejunum/Ileum dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 – Distant Metastatic Disease

options.label: Palliative diversion (if obstructed)
Proceed to Metastatic Adenocarcinoma workup and systemic therapy (SBA-8)
options.recommendation: Biomarker testing; checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is an option for any line.

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: No adjuvant therapy[SBA-6].

nodes.criteria: T3, N0, M0 (stage IIA) without high-risk features

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation is standard; consider fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine as an option[SBA-6].

options.label: Consider fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine (6 months)
options.recommendation: Especially if high-risk features present per clinician judgment.

nodes.criteria: T3, N0, M0 with high-risk features (close/positive margins, <5 lymph nodes for duodenal or <8 for jejunal/ileal, tumor perforation) or T4, N0, M0 (stage IIB)

options.label: FOLFOX or CAPEOX (3–6 months)
options.recommendation: Extrapolation from colon cancer data; note no SBA patients included in IDEA trial[SBA-6 footnote n]. For duodenal positive margin, consider sequential chemo/RT with capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil[SBA-6 footnote o], [SBA-E].

options.label: Fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine (6 months)
options.recommendation: If oxaliplatin not tolerated or contraindicated.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy recommended for high-risk stage II; chemo/RT for positive margin duodenal.

options.label: FOLFOX or CAPEOX (3–6 months)
options.recommendation: Oxaliplatin-based therapy; for duodenal positive margin, consider sequential chemo/RT[SBA-6].

options.label: Fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine (6 months)
options.recommendation: If oxaliplatin not suitable.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy for all stage III; clinical trial encouraged.

management_pathways.title: Adjuvant Treatment – dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 (Duodenum or Jejunum/Ileum)
branching_basis: MMR/MSI status: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated (TMB>50 mut/Mb)

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation is standard due to favorable prognosis[SBA-7].

options.label: FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab
options.recommendation: Atezolizumab given for 12 cycles (6 months) then maintenance for 12 months total[SBA-7], [SBA-D 4]. Alternatively, FOLFOX or CAPEOX alone (3–6 months) may be used[SBA-7].

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: May be considered if no high-risk features.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemo-immunotherapy recommended for T4N0; role of adjuvant immunotherapy after neoadjuvant immunotherapy is not defined[SBA-7 footnote q].

options.label: FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab
options.recommendation: Same schedule as for T4N0[SBA-7], [SBA-D 4].

options.label: FOLFOX or CAPEOX (3–6 months) alone
options.recommendation: If immunotherapy contraindicated or not desired[SBA-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemo-immunotherapy recommended for stage III dMMR/MSI-H SBA.

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Adenocarcinoma – Workup and Systemic Therapy
branching_basis: Biomarker profile (RAS, BRAF, HER2, MMR, TMB, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C)

nodes.criteria: Newly diagnosed metastatic or recurrence after curative therapy

options.label: Biomarker testing performed as rapidly as possible
options.recommendation: RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification; MMR or MSI status; multigene panel testing for POLE/POLD1, RET, NTRK 1/2/3, and TMB[SBA-8].

options.label: Imaging: chest/abdomen/pelvis CT; consider FDG-PET/CT if equivocal
options.recommendation: CT with IV contrast; MR if contraindication; FDG-PET/CT not routine but may help in equivocal cases[SBA-A].

options.label: Palliative diversion/stent if obstructed
options.recommendation: For symptomatic obstruction.

options.label: Initial therapy – Intensive therapy recommended
options.recommendation: FOLFOX, CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, or FOLFIRINOX ± bevacizumab. If BRAF V600E mutation: (FOLFOX/Encorafenib + [Cetuximab or Panitumumab])[SBA-D 1].

options.label: Initial therapy – Intensive therapy NOT recommended
options.preference: for poor PS or older age
options.recommendation: Capecitabine ± bevacizumab or Fluorouracil/Leucovorin ± bevacizumab[SBA-D 1].

options.label: Initial therapy if prior FOLFOX/CAPEOX adjuvant within 12 months or contraindication
options.recommendation: FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab; Taxane-based chemotherapy; If BRAF V600E: Dabrafenib/Trametinib or (FOLFOX/Encorafenib + [Cetuximab or Panitumumab]); If TMB-H (>10 mut/Mb): Pembrolizumab (category 2A)[SBA-D 1].

nodes.criteria: Second-line and subsequent therapy (if not previously given)

options.label: Chemotherapy options
options.recommendation: CAPEOX, FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, Irinotecan – all ± bevacizumab; Taxane-based chemotherapy[SBA-D 1].

options.label: Biomarker-directed options
options.recommendation: BRAF V600E: Dabrafenib/Trametinib, Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab), or (FOLFOX/Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab)) (if no prior oxaliplatin/BRAF-targeted)[SBA-D 1 footnote l]. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+): Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki; or if RAS/BRAF WT: (Pertuzumab or Tucatinib) + Trastuzumab. TMB-H (>10 mut/Mb): Pembrolizumab (category 2A). NTRK 1/2/3 fusion: Entrectinib, Larotrectinib, or Repotrectinib. RET fusion: Selpercatinib. KRAS G12C: (Sotorasib or Adagrasib) ± (Cetuximab or Panitumumab)[SBA-D 1].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: When no further active therapy is appropriate.

options.label: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy
options.recommendation: PD-1: Cemiplimab, Dostarlimab, Nivolumab, Pembrolizumab, Penpulimab, Retifanlimab, Tislelizumab, Toripalimab. PD-L1: Atezolizumab, Avelumab, Cosibelimab, Durvalumab. PD-1/CTLA-4: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab. Some agents have not been specifically studied in SBA but are included based on presumed class effect[SBA-D 2]. See SBA-D 7-9 for dosing.

items.text: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with IV iodinated and oral contrast (unless contraindicated) to assess local tumor extent and distant metastases to lungs, lymph nodes, liver, peritoneum[SBA-A 1].

If IV contrast contraindicated, MR abdomen/pelvis with IV gadolinium-based contrast may be obtained (gadobutrol, gadopentetate dimeglumine, gadobenate dimeglumine, or gadoteridol in selected patients with chronic kidney failure)[SBA-A 1].
Consider CT enterography when primary tumor is poorly visualized by standard methods[SBA-A 1].
MR enterography may be considered when there is contraindication to CT[SBA-A 1].
MRCP may be obtained in initial workup of suspected duodenal malignancies to ascertain tumor site of origin, especially with biliary obstruction[SBA-A 1].

FDG-PET/CT is not routinely indicated; may be considered for equivocal CT/MR findings or evaluation of potential peritoneal disease where lesions are sized greater than lower limits of FDG-PET detection[SBA-A 1].
EGD for detection and pathologic sampling when duodenal malignancy is suspected; palliative stenting if obstruction detected[SBA-A 2].

EUS for enhanced pre-therapeutic clinical staging of proximal small intestinal malignancies and to discern duodenal from ampullary, biliary, or pancreatic primaries; also consider if other radiologic imaging insufficient for clinical staging[SBA-1 footnote d], [SBA-A 2].
Push- or device-assisted enteroscopy (double- or single-balloon) not routine; may be considered for patients with small intestinal strictures for diagnostic/palliative benefit[SBA-A 2].

Capsule endoscopy: consider when radiographic imaging and other endoscopy fail to reveal suspected primary lesion; not preferred primary method due to inability to obtain tissue. Contraindicated in small bowel obstruction or strictures[SBA-A 2].
Complete blood count (CBC), chemistry profile, CA 19-9, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA)[SBA-1].
MMR or MSI testing in all newly diagnosed patients (universal testing)[SBA-B].

For metastatic/recurrent disease: RAS and BRAF V600E mutations; HER2 (ERBB2) overexpression/amplification; MMR or MSI if not previously done; multigene panel testing for POLE/POLD1, RET, NTRK 1/2/3, and TMB[SBA-8].
DPYD testing for fluoropyrimidine-associated toxicity (see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer)[SBA-1 footnote b].
Prefer pathologic confirmation before resection when possible[SBA-B].

Report primary tumor site (duodenum, jejunum, ileum, overlapping, NOS), grade, depth of invasion (T), number of lymph nodes evaluated and positive (N), margin status (proximal, distal, radial/mesenteric, uncinate, bile duct, pancreas, other), lymphovascular invasion, MSI/MMR status, evidence of celiac disease, presence of Crohn's disease, presence of polyps[SBA-B].

For duodenal tumors in second portion, differentiate from ampullary malignancies: epicenter of tumor or precursor lesion should not be at ampulla, and >75% of mass should not be within ampulla[SBA-B].

Lymph node evaluation: AJCC recommends minimum 8 nodes; regional nodes differ by site – duodenum: retropancreatic, hepatic artery, inferior pancreaticoduodenal, superior mesenteric; jejunum/ileum: cecal (terminal ileum only), ileocolic (terminal ileum only), superior mesenteric, mesenteric, NOS[SBA-B].

All patients with SBA should be counseled for familial malignancies and considered for risk assessment of Lynch syndrome (HNPCC), FAP, PJS, and other polypoid mutations; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric[SBA-1 footnote f].
Distress screening: refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, including social determinants of health (NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management)[SBA-1 footnote c].
Consider studies for celiac disease or Crohn's disease depending on tumor location and patient history[SBA-1].
For patients with obstructive symptoms, consider palliative diversion or stenting as part of initial evaluation[SBA-2], [SBA-3], [SBA-4], [SBA-5].

surgery.role: Surgery is the only curative treatment for localized SBA. The primary goal is complete (R0) resection of the primary tumor with adequate lymphadenectomy (≥8 lymph nodes). Surgical approach is determined by anatomic subsite, tumor extent, and patient factors. Diagnostic laparoscopy is not routinely indicated for clearly resectable early-stage disease but may be considered when occult peritoneal or metastatic disease is suspected (locally advanced tumors, high-risk histology, elevated tumor markers, or discordant findings) and when results would alter management[SBA-C].
principles: Intraoperative staging of the abdomen (mesentery, omentum, peritoneum) should be completed in all cases[SBA-C].

Adequate lymph node dissection with retrieval and evaluation of at least 8 lymph nodes (AJCC recommendation) is a goal for all resections. The number of nodes examined is a strong prognostic factor[SBA-C], [SBA-B].
Diagnostic laparoscopy not routine for early-stage resectable disease; consider for specific scenarios where occult metastases suspected and management would be altered[SBA-C].
Margins: for duodenal resections, consider frozen section if concerns; if margins <5 mm, consider re-excision of involved margin[SBA-C].
Specimen orientation and inking involve both surgeon and pathologist to ensure accurate assessment of size, extent, and margin status[SBA-B].

procedures.name: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure)
procedures.approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic (minimally invasive should only be used by experienced surgeons). Pylorus preservation acceptable in absence of hereditary condition[SBA-C].
procedures.indications: All duodenal malignancies, particularly those arising in second portion of duodenum or invading any portion of ampulla or pancreas[SBA-C].
procedures.key_complications: Pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying, hemorrhage, infection, bile leak, pancreatic insufficiency, neuropathy from T3/4 dissection[SBA-C].

procedures.name: Limited segmentectomy (duodenum)
procedures.approach: Open. Resection of involved segment with primary anastomosis.
procedures.indications: Select cases for lesions on anti-mesenteric side involving third and fourth segments of duodenum, or for lesions in first portion of duodenum <2 cm on mesenteric side. Controversial; lower lymph node yield reported[SBA-C], [SBA-2].
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, inadequate lymph node harvest, recurrence[SBA-C].

procedures.name: Segmentectomy (jejunum/ileum)
procedures.approach: Open or laparoscopic. Lymph nodes identified and resected down to origin of feeder vessels. Clinically suspicious nodes outside field of resection should be biopsied or resected if possible[SBA-C].
procedures.indications: Standard for jejunal and ileal adenocarcinomas. Margins of at least 5–10 cm on either side of tumor[SBA-C].

procedures.name: Terminal ileal resection with right hemicolectomy
procedures.indications: Distal ileal tumors (terminal ileum). Includes resection of cecum and ascending colon to ensure adequate lymphadenectomy[SBA-C].
procedures.key_complications: Anastomotic leak, infection, diarrhea, bile salt malabsorption[SBA-C].

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by the ability to achieve R0 removal of all disease with adequate function. For duodenal tumors, invasion of superior mesenteric artery, superior mesenteric vein beyond reconstruction, or distant metastases preclude upfront resection. For jejunum/ileum, locally advanced tumors may be unresectable due to involvement of mesenteric root or distant metastases. In cases of borderline resectability, neoadjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation may be used to convert to resectable disease. Diagnostic laparoscopy may help identify occult peritoneal metastases. Potentially resectable visceral or peritoneal metastases are extremely rare for SBA; metastasectomy may be considered for highly select patients after multidisciplinary evaluation[SBA-8 footnote r].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is used primarily for duodenal adenocarcinoma. It is not generally indicated for jejunum/ileum primaries; any consideration for these sites must be made on a highly selected basis by a multidisciplinary team[SBA-E]. For duodenal cancer, RT is considered in the following settings: (1) adjuvant sequential chemoradiation after systemic therapy for margin-positive resections; (2) preoperative chemoradiation for patients who remain unresectable after a course of induction chemotherapy; (3) primary chemoradiation for locally unresectable disease as part of management. Database analysis suggests no survival benefit from adjuvant chemo/RT versus chemotherapy alone in patients with surgically resected duodenal adenocarcinoma[SBA-E].
principles: Fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy should be delivered concurrently with RT (capecitabine 825 mg/m² PO BID on radiation days, or infusional fluorouracil 225 mg/m²/day continuous infusion during RT)[SBA-D 4], [SBA-D 6].
Treatment can be delivered using 3D-CRT. When appropriate, advanced treatment planning such as IMRT should be considered to limit toxicity to adjacent normal organs[SBA-E].
Image-guided RT (IGRT) with kV imaging, MR-guided imaging, or cone beam CT should be routinely used during IMRT[SBA-E].
Target volumes should include the primary site and regional lymph node basins (e.g., retropancreatic, hepatic artery, inferior pancreaticoduodenal, superior mesenteric for duodenum)[SBA-E].
Patients should be evaluated by multidisciplinary teams at high-volume centers when RT is considered[SBA-E].

dose_frameworks.name: Standard fractionation for duodenal RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant (margin-positive), preoperative (unresectable after induction), or definitive for duodenal primary. Dose determined by tolerance of adjacent normal tissues[SBA-E].

approaches.name: Adjuvant sequential chemoradiation for duodenal cancer
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine 825 mg/m² PO BID on radiation days or infusional fluorouracil 225 mg/m²/day continuous infusion during RT[SBA-D 4]
approaches.indication: Stage II–III duodenal pMMR/MSS or dMMR/MSI-H with positive margins after resection, following systemic therapy[SBA-6 footnotes n and o], [SBA-7 footnote o]
approaches.key_trial: No SBA-specific phase III data; extrapolation from rectal cancer studies (Allegra et al., JNCI 2015)[SBA-D 4 reference 4]
approaches.toxicities: Acute: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dermatitis, fatigue, myelosuppression. Late: small bowel stricture, enteritis, fistula (dose constraints: adjacent small bowel Dmax 55 Gy, V45Gy ≤150 cc, V50 ≤30 cc for individual loops if possible)[SBA-E].

approaches.name: Preoperative chemoradiation for initially unresectable duodenal cancer
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Capecitabine or infusional fluorouracil as above
approaches.indication: Locally unresectable duodenal adenocarcinoma after induction chemotherapy, to achieve conversion to resectable disease[SBA-2 footnote i], [SBA-3 footnote i], [SBA-E]
approaches.key_trial: Small retrospective series (Onkendi et al., J Gastrointest Surg 2012)[SBA-E reference 2]; prospective phase II (Yeung et al., Cancer 1993)[MS-9]
approaches.toxicities: Similar to above; includes radiation-induced edema, pancreatitis if pancreas included in field.

approaches.name: Definitive chemoradiation for medically inoperable duodenal cancer
approaches.indication: Medically inoperable or unresectable duodenal cancer without metastases
approaches.key_trial: Limited data; considered when systemic therapy alone insufficient
approaches.toxicities: Acute and late as above; may require dose reduction if adjacent small bowel dose constraints cannot be met.

systemic_therapy.overview: Many systemic therapy regimens for SBA are extrapolated from data for colorectal cancer (CRC) due to the rarity of SBA and lack of dedicated trials. The NCCN Panel strongly encourages enrollment in clinical trials for all SBA patients[MS-1]. Systemic therapy is used in the adjuvant setting (after resection of localized disease) and in advanced/metastatic disease. The choice of regimen depends on MMR/MSI status, biomarker profile (RAS, BRAF V600E, HER2, NTRK, RET, KRAS G12C, TMB), prior therapy, performance status, and treatment goals (adjuvant vs. palliative). For advanced disease, initial intensive therapy is recommended for patients with good PS (ECOG 0–2) and includes oxaliplatin- or irinotecan-based doublets/triplets ± bevacizumab. Non-intensive therapy (fluoropyrimidine alone ± bevacizumab) is for patients who are not candidates for intensive therapy. For dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated tumors, checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy is preferred at any line[SBA-D 1-2]. Bevacizumab has been shown to be safe in advanced SBA, although efficacy has not been proven; an FDA-approved biosimilar may be substituted[SBA-D 3 footnote f,c]. Oxaliplatin discontinuation after 3–4 months (or sooner for neurotoxicity) is recommended, while maintaining other agents[SBA-D 3 footnote e].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Stage III: FOLFOX or CAPEOX for 3–6 months. High-risk stage II (T4N0 or T3N0 with high-risk features): FOLFOX or CAPEOX for 3–6 months. T3N0 no high-risk: observation or fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolation from colon cancer data (IDEA trial) showing noninferiority of 3 months CAPEOX for 3-year DFS in stage III colon cancer. No SBA patients in IDEA, but reasonable in absence of direct data[SBA-6 footnote n]. Adjuvant chemotherapy benefit in SBA remains controversial, with conflicting retrospective analyses[SBA-6 footnote t].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fluorouracil/leucovorin or capecitabine (6 months) for patients who cannot tolerate oxaliplatin.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Stage IIB (T4N0) and stage III: FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab for 6 months (induction) then maintenance atezolizumab for additional 6 months (total 12 months immunotherapy)[SBA-7].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on the ATOMIC trial (Alliance A021502) in stage III dMMR colon cancer showing benefit of adding atezolizumab to FOLFOX[SBA-D 4 reference 5]. Role of adjuvant immunotherapy after neoadjuvant immunotherapy is not defined[SBA-7 footnote q].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: FOLFOX or CAPEOX alone (3–6 months) for patients unsuitable for immunotherapy.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic – Initial therapy (pMMR/MSS, no biomarker-driven option)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: FOLFOX ± bevacizumab, CAPEOX ± bevacizumab, FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab, or FOLFIRINOX ± bevacizumab (category 2A)[SBA-D 1].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase II studies show ORR 45–50% for CAPEOX and FOLFOX in metastatic SBA[MS-12]. FOLFIRINOX extrapolated from CAPIRINOX trial[SBA-D 5 reference 15,16]. Bevacizumab safety data from retrospective series[SBA-D 3 footnote f].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Non-intensive: Capecitabine ± bevacizumab or Fluorouracil/Leucovorin ± bevacizumab. If prior adjuvant oxaliplatin within 12 months: FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab, or taxane-based chemo.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic – Initial therapy (BRAF V600E mutation, pMMR/MSS)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolated from CRC BEACON trial data. Encorafenib + cetuximab with FOLFOX as initial therapy (category 2A)[SBA-D 1].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Standard FOLFOX/CAPEOX ± bevacizumab if BRAF-targeted therapy not available.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy: PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, dostarlimab, etc.) or PD-L1 (atezolizumab, avelumab, etc.) or CTLA-4 combination (ipilimumab + nivolumab)[SBA-D 2].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: KEYNOTE-158, ZEBRA, CheckMate-142 studies show high ORR and durable responses in dMMR/MSI-H SBA. FDA-approved for this population[MS-13], [MS-14].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: If immunotherapy contraindicated: FOLFOX, CAPEOX, or FOLFIRINOX.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: If first-line was oxaliplatin-based and no progression within 12 months: re-challenge with FOLFOX/CAPEOX ± bevacizumab. Otherwise: FOLFIRI ± bevacizumab, Irinotecan ± bevacizumab, or taxane-based chemotherapy[SBA-D 1].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Retrospective data support FOLFIRI after first-line platinum (ORR 20%, DCR 52%)[MS-17]. Taxane-based retrospective series show disease control in 65%[MS-17].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Biomarker-directed options per testing results (see below).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Metastatic – Biomarker-directed second-line or subsequent therapy
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: BRAF V600E: Dabrafenib/Trametinib (FDA-approved for BRAF V600E solid tumors, ORR 67% in small intestine cohort of ROAR trial) or Encorafenib + (Cetuximab or Panitumumab) ± FOLFOX if no prior oxaliplatin[SBA-D 1], [MS-15]. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+): Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (ORR 61.3% in IHC 3+ tumors in DESTINY-PanTumor02)[SBA-D 1], [MS-16]. HER2 overexpression/amplification with RAS/BRAF WT: (Pertuzumab or Tucatinib) + Trastuzumab. TMB-H (>10 mut/Mb): Pembrolizumab (category 2A). NTRK 1/2/3 fusion: Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, or Repotrectinib[MS-17], [MS-18]. RET fusion: Selpercatinib (complete response in small bowel cancer in LIBRETTO-001)[MS-18]. KRAS G12C: (Sotorasib or Adagrasib) ± (Cetuximab or Panitumumab)[SBA-D 1].
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Tumor-agnostic approvals and basket trials support these targeted therapies in biomarker-selected SBA.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Consider best supportive care if no further active options.

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX (mFOLFOX6)

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m² IV bolus on Day 1, then 1200 mg/m²/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m² over 46–48 hours) continuous infusion

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neuropathy (oxaliplatin), neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, fatigue, stomatitis[SBA-D 4], [SBA-D 5].

key_regimens.name: CAPEOX
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neuropathy (oxaliplatin), hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, nausea, fatigue. Note: North American patients may require lower capecitabine dose due to increased toxicity[SBA-D 4 footnote w].

key_regimens.name: FOLFIRI

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m² IV bolus Day 1, then 1200 mg/m²/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m² over 46–48 hours) continuous infusion

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Delayed diarrhea, neutropenia, alopecia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, stomatitis[SBA-D 5].

key_regimens.name: FOLFIRINOX

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.dose: 400 mg/m² IV push Day 1, then 1200 mg/m²/day x 2 days (total 2400 mg/m² over 46 hours) continuous infusion

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, diarrhea, neuropathy, fatigue. Modified FOLFIRINOX (irinotecan 150 mg/m²) is an option to reduce toxicity[SBA-D 5]. FOLFOXIRI not recommended due to high fluorouracil dose (3200 mg/m²) – greater toxicity in US patients[SBA-D 5 footnote y].

key_regimens.name: FOLFOX + Atezolizumab (adjuvant)

drugs.name: Atezolizumab
drugs.day: Day 1 (q2w) for 12 cycles (6 months), then maintenance atezolizumab 840 mg q2w for 13 cycles (12 months total)

key_regimens.cycle_days: 14 days for induction; 14 days for maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatitis) plus FOLFOX toxicities[SBA-D 4].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic (dMMR/MSI-H, POLE/POLD1, or TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb) – any line

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV every 3 weeks OR 400 mg IV every 6 weeks; also 2 mg/kg q3w or 4 mg/kg q6w. Subcutaneous preparation (pembrolizumab + hyaluronidase) may be substituted[SBA-D 7], [SBA-D 3 footnote k].

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, hypothyroidism, rash, fatigue. Monitor for infusion reactions[SBA-D 3 footnote i].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic dMMR/MSI-H (as subsequent therapy if prior checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy). Not for initial therapy per standard, but listed as option[SBA-D 2, D-3 footnote s].

drugs.name: Then Nivolumab maintenance
drugs.dose: 3 mg/kg IV or 240 mg IV q2w

key_regimens.cycle_days: 21 days for induction; 14 days for maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Higher rate of immune-related adverse events than monotherapy, including colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, rash. CheckPoint inhibitor toxicity management per NCCN Guidelines[SBA-D 3 footnote i].

key_regimens.name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki)
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+) after prior therapy
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Interstitial lung disease (ILD)/pneumonitis (3.5% drug-related deaths on DESTINY-CRC01), neutropenia, nausea, fatigue. Caution in patients with underlying lung issues[SBA-D 3 footnote n], [SBA-D 8].

key_regimens.name: Encorafenib + Cetuximab
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic BRAF V600E mutation (second-line or subsequent)

drugs.name: Cetuximab
drugs.dose: 500 mg/m² IV every 2 weeks OR 400 mg/m² IV followed by 250 mg/m² IV weekly

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Rash, diarrhea, fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain. Cetuximab: infusion reaction, hypomagnesemia[SBA-D 9].

key_regimens.name: Dabrafenib + Trametinib
key_regimens.setting: Metastatic BRAF V600E mutation (second-line or subsequent)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Pyrexia, fatigue, nausea, rash, chills. Serious AEs: hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, cardiomyopathy, skin toxicity. FDA-approved for BRAF V600E solid tumors[SBA-D 8].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment for Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment is performed at multiple time points: after completion of neoadjuvant therapy (to evaluate conversion to resectability), after surgery (baseline for adjuvant decision), during adjuvant therapy (not routinely; mainly to monitor toxicity), during palliative systemic therapy for advanced/metastatic disease (typically every 2–3 months or after 2–4 cycles), after completion of adjuvant therapy (to establish new baseline for surveillance), and when clinical suspicion of recurrence arises. For patients undergoing systemic therapy for advanced disease, imaging (chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast) is recommended every 8–12 weeks or per clinical need[SBA-A 2]. Biomarker levels (CEA and CA 19-9) may be checked at the time of imaging, though no prospective data support tight interval substitution for imaging.
response_logic: For neoadjuvant therapy (induction chemo or chemo/RT): After 2–4 months, repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to assess response. If conversion to resectable occurs, proceed to surgery. If stable disease or progression, consider alternative systemic therapy or RT (especially for duodenal primaries with positive margins). Preoperative chemo/RT should be considered in patients who remain unresectable after induction chemotherapy[SBA-2 footnote i], [SBA-E].

For first-line palliative therapy: Imaging after 2–3 months (e.g., after 4 cycles of FOLFOX). If response or stable disease, continue therapy (with consideration of oxaliplatin break after 3–4 months). If progression, switch to second-line therapy. If mixed response, consider local ablative options for limited progression. FDG-PET/CT is not routinely indicated but may be considered when CT/MR results are equivocal[SBA-A 1].

For immunotherapy: Response may be delayed or pseudoprogression; confirm progression on subsequent imaging if clinical stability. Continue immunotherapy until confirmed progression, unacceptable toxicity, or up to 2 years (for pembrolizumab, per FDA label).

For biomarker-directed therapy: Assess target lesions with CT/MR every 2–3 cycles. For targeted agents with known high response rates (e.g., dabrafenib/trametinib, selpercatinib), early response is common. Confirm response at first assessment.

After curative-intent surgery and adjuvant therapy: No response assessment beyond baseline staging is required; routine surveillance for recurrence per schedule (H&P, CEA/CA19-9, CT every 6–12 months for 2 years, then annually)[SBA-6], [SBA-7].
imaging_recommendations: Use same modality (CT vs. MR) for consistent comparison. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (oral contrast as needed) is standard[SBA-A 1].
If CT contraindicated, MR abdomen/pelvis with IV gadolinium-based contrast is acceptable[SBA-A 1].
CT enterography not routinely indicated for monitoring; reserved for clinical necessity[SBA-A 2].

FDG-PET/CT is not indicated for routine surveillance or response assessment; may be considered for equivocal findings on CT/MR and when detection of peritoneal disease is important (lesions > lower limits of FDG detection)[SBA-A 1].

In patients with serial biomarker elevation (CEA, CA 19-9) but no evidence of disease on CT, consider FDG-PET/CT and endoscopic evaluation. If still negative, repeat CT and biomarkers in 3 months[SBA-8].
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If recurrence is suspected based on imaging or biomarker elevation, tissue biopsy (endoscopic or CT-guided) is recommended to confirm recurrence and to reassess biomarker status (MMR/MSI, RAS, BRAF, HER2, etc.) as molecular profile may change. For oligometastatic recurrence (e.g., solitary liver or lung metastasis), consider metastasectomy after multidisciplinary evaluation[SBA-8 footnote r].

Potentially resectable visceral or peritoneal metastases are extremely rare for SBA. If considered, evaluation by a surgeon experienced in metastasectomy and/or cytoreductive surgery (CRS) is required. HIPEC is not recommended due to lack of evidence and significant morbidity[SBA-8 footnote r], [MS-10]. Proceed to systemic therapy per metastatic guidelines if unresectable.

If a patient with localized disease had R1 resection (microscopic positive margin) and received adjuvant chemo/RT, and then develops local recurrence, consider re-resection if feasible, otherwise systemic therapy.

For patients who progress on first-line immunotherapy, consider switching to chemotherapy (FOLFOX/CAPEOX/FOLFIRINOX) if not previously given, or enrollment in clinical trial. For progression on chemotherapy, explore second-line options including clinical trials and biomarker-directed therapy.
Best supportive care is an option at any point when disease is refractory and no further active therapy is appropriate.

prognosis.overall: Small bowel adenocarcinoma (SBA) has a rising incidence, with an estimated 12,440 new cases and 2,090 deaths in the United States in 2024 [1]. The overall 5-year relative survival for all stages combined is approximately 65-70%, with localized disease (stage I-III) having a 5-year survival of 85% and distant metastatic (stage IV) disease a 5-year survival of only 42% [105]. SBA is often diagnosed at a higher stage than colorectal cancer, contributing to worse stage-for-stage survival. The median age at diagnosis is 66 years, with a slight male predominance (incidence 2.6 per 100,000 for males vs. 2.0 for females) [2,40].

by_stage.context: Based on SEER data for localized SBA (stage I-III) [105]. Specific stage I survival not explicitly separated but prognosis is excellent with complete resection.

by_stage.stage: Stage IIA (T3, N0, M0) and IIB (T4, N0, M0)
by_stage.context: Stage IIA no high-risk features may have observation alone; stage IIB (T4) considered high-risk and often considered for adjuvant therapy [SBA-6]. dMMR/MSI-H stage II patients may have improved prognosis but this is extrapolated from colorectal cancer data [SBA-B].

by_stage.stage: Stage IIIA (any T, N1, M0) and IIIB (any T, N2, M0)
by_stage.context: Stage III disease with lymph node involvement has significantly worse outcomes compared to stage I-II. Adjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFOX or CAPEOX) is recommended for stage III pMMR/MSS tumors [SBA-6]. Five-year survival for stage III is not explicitly given but recurrence rates after surgery are high [36].

by_stage.stage: Stage IV (any T, any N, M1)
by_stage.context: Distant metastatic disease. Median OS in clinical trials: with FOLFOX, median OS 17.3 months [128]; with CAPEOX, median OS 17.3 months [126]; with pembrolizumab in MSI-H SBA, median OS not reached at time of publication (KEYNOTE-158) [142]. Peritoneal carcinomatosis carries particularly poor prognosis with median OS 5.9 months [109].

prognostic_factors: Lymph node status: number of positive nodes (N1 vs N2) and lymph node ratio [60-62]
Lymph node harvest: <5 nodes for duodenal, <8 for jejunal/ileal tumors considered inadequate [40,60,61]
Margin status: positive margins (R1/R2) worsen prognosis [SBA-6]
MSI/MMR status: dMMR/MSI-H may be associated with better prognosis in stage II (extrapolated from CRC) but also predicts response to immunotherapy [SBA-B]
Primary tumor site: duodenal tumors may have different outcomes than jejunal/ileal [36]
Genetic alterations: BRAF V600E, RAS mutations, HER2 amplification, POLE/POLD1 mutations [35]
Tumor mutational burden (TMB): TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) found in 9-11% of SBA, associated with immunotherapy response [35,155]
Inflammatory conditions: Crohn's disease and celiac disease confer increased risk but impact on prognosis not fully defined [11,12,17]
prognosis.prognostic_context: SBA is a rare cancer with limited prospective data; many prognostic factors are extrapolated from colorectal cancer. The NCCN SBA guideline recommends MMR/MSI testing universally for all newly diagnosed patients due to its prognostic and predictive value [SBA-B]. Stage-for-stage, SBA has worse survival than colon cancer due to more advanced presentation and limited treatment data [58,59]. Participation in clinical trials is strongly encouraged given the dearth of evidence.

disease_related.management: Palliative diversion, stenting (endoscopic or surgical bypass). Obstruction is common, accounting for 22-57.9% of presentations [41,98-100]. Stent placement may be considered for duodenal obstruction; surgical bypass for jejunal/ileal obstruction [SBA-2, SBA-4].
Palliative diversion or stent [SBA-2]. Endoscopic duodenal stenting may provide relief; however, survival after stenting is limited [101].
Occurs in approximately 25-33% of cases [36,41]. Endoscopic evaluation, transfusion support, and definitive treatment of primary tumor.

Affects 25-50% of stage IV patients [108]. Surgical cytoreduction may be considered for resectable disease; systemic therapy for unresectable. HIPEC is not recommended due to lack of evidence [109-115]. Median OS 5.9 months [109].
MRCP may be needed to distinguish duodenal from ampullary/pancreatic primaries [SBA-A]. Endoscopic drainage or stenting may be required.
Nutritional support, dietary modification. Consider celiac disease evaluation in appropriate patients.

chemotherapy: Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy: may limit duration of therapy. Discontinuation of oxaliplatin should be strongly considered after 3-4 months (or sooner for unacceptable neurotoxicity) from FOLFOX/CAPEOX. Duloxetine, pregabalin, gabapentin are ineffective for numbness/tingling; only considered if pain present. Non-pharmacologic interventions (acupuncture, ice/heat, physical activity) [SBA-D 3, SBA-F]

Fluoropyrimidine toxicity: DPYD testing recommended prior to capecitabine or 5-FU initiation to identify patients at risk for severe toxicity [SBA-1 footnote b, SBA-D 3 footnote d]. North American patients may experience greater toxicity than European patients [SBA-D 4 footnote w]
Myelosuppression: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia common with FOLFOX/CAPEOX [126-128]
Gastrointestinal: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. See supportive care for antiemetic protocols.
Cardiac: 5-FU can cause coronary vasospasm; monitor for chest pain.
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs): rash, pruritus, thyroiditis, hypothyroidism, hypophysitis, pancreatitis, pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis. Management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [SBA-D 3 footnote i]
Infusion reactions: for pembrolizumab, atezolizumab subcutaneous formulations available [SBA-D 3 footnote k, r]
Interstitial lung disease: fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan carries a 3.5% risk of drug-related death from ILD; caution in patients with underlying lung issues [SBA-D 3 footnote n]
Nivolumab + ipilimumab: may be considered as subsequent therapy if checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy was previously received [SBA-D 3 footnote s]
targeted_therapy: BRAF/MEK inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib): pyrexia (40.8%), fatigue, chills, nausea, rash. Serious AEs in 22.3%, fatal in 4.4% [159]
Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan: nausea (55.1%), anemia, diarrhea, vomiting, fatigue. ILD/pneumonitis in 20.5% [162]. Grade ≥3 AEs in 40.8% [162]
NTRK inhibitors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib): AEs mostly grade 1-2; dizziness common with repotrectinib [168-170]
RET inhibitor (selpercatinib): hypertension, transaminitis, drug-induced liver injury [171]
surgery: Postoperative complications: pancreatic fistula after pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple), wound infection, delayed gastric emptying, bleeding. Minimally invasive procedures should only be used by experienced surgeons [SBA-C]
Inadequate lymph node harvest: <8 lymph nodes associated with worse staging and outcomes [SBA-C, SBA-B]
Positive margins: consider re-excision if margins <5 mm; for duodenal cancers, sequential chemo/RT may be considered [SBA-6, SBA-7]
Morbidity rates: higher with radical resection (Whipple) vs segmental duodenectomy; limited resection may have better morbidity profile [68,69]
Diagnostic laparoscopy: not routinely indicated for resectable early-stage disease; may be considered when occult peritoneal disease is suspected [SBA-C]
radiation: Acute toxicity: duodenitis, enteritis, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy given concurrently with RT [SBA-E]
Late toxicity: small bowel obstruction, fistula, chronic enteritis. Dose constraints: adjacent small bowel Dmax 55 Gy, V45 Gy ≤150 cc, V50 ≤30 cc for individual loops if possible [SBA-E reference 3]
RT is generally not indicated for jejunal/ileal primary tumors; any consideration must be on highly selected basis [SBA-E]
IMRT and IGRT should be used to limit toxicity to normal organs [SBA-E]

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care for SBA patients largely follows principles established for colorectal cancer, given the rarity of SBA-specific data. Key elements include nutritional support, antiemetic management, growth factor support, VTE prophylaxis, pain management, psychosocial support, and dental care. All patients should be screened for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [SBA-1 footnote c]. DPYD testing should be considered before starting fluoropyrimidine therapy [SBA-1 footnote b, SBA-D 3 footnote d].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Patients with SBA are at high risk for malnutrition due to bowel obstruction, malabsorption (especially in duodenal primaries and after Whipple surgery), and treatment side effects. Nutritional assessment and intervention (dietary counseling, oral supplements, or enteral/parenteral nutrition) should be provided. For patients with celiac disease, strict gluten avoidance is critical [SBA-F]. After pancreaticoduodenectomy, pancreatic enzyme replacement may be needed. Cachexia should be managed with a multidisciplinary approach including dietitian, physical therapy, and psychological support.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Standard prophylaxis for moderate-to-high emetic risk chemotherapy (carboplatin, oxaliplatin, irinotecan) includes a neurokinin-1 (NK1) receptor antagonist, a serotonin (5-HT3) receptor antagonist, and dexamethasone. For low emetic risk regimens (fluorouracil, capecitabine), a 5-HT3 antagonist or dexamethasone alone may be sufficient. The NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis should be followed. Prophylaxis for delayed emesis is particularly important with oxaliplatin-based regimens.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Primary prophylaxis with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (GCSF) is recommended for regimens with a high risk (>20%) of febrile neutropenia. For SBA, standard regimens (FOLFOX, FOLFOXIRI) have moderate risk of neutropenia. The NCCN Guidelines for Myeloid Growth Factors should be consulted for specific recommendations based on regimen and patient risk factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Cancer-associated thrombosis prevention should follow established guidelines. For ambulatory patients with metastatic SBA on active systemic therapy, consider prophylactic anticoagulation (low molecular weight heparin or direct oral anticoagulants) based on Khorana risk score or other validated tools. Patients undergoing major abdominal surgery (Whipple, segmentectomy) should receive perioperative VTE prophylaxis as per standard surgical protocols.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain in SBA may arise from tumor obstruction, peritoneal involvement, neuropathic pain (oxaliplatin), or postoperative causes. For neuropathic pain from oxaliplatin, duloxetine, pregabalin, or gabapentin may be used only if pain is present; they are ineffective for numbness or tingling [SBA-F]. Non-pharmacologic interventions such as acupuncture, heat/ice, and balanced physical activity should be considered [SBA-F]. See NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain for comprehensive management.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: All patients should undergo distress screening using the NCCN Distress Thermometer. Survivorship care planning includes addressing emotional distress, social concerns, financial toxicity, and sexual dysfunction. Referral to social work, psychology, or psychiatry as needed. Support groups for rare cancers may be beneficial. See NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management.
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation before initiating chemotherapy is recommended to reduce the risk of oral mucositis and infection. Good oral hygiene, fluoride treatments, and management of xerostomia may help during treatment with 5-FU or capecitabine. For patients receiving bisphosphonates or denosumab (not typically used in SBA), dental assessment is essential to prevent osteonecrosis of the jaw.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After curative-intent surgery and adjuvant chemotherapy (if administered), follow-up surveillance is recommended to detect recurrence and manage treatment-related complications. The NCCN Panel recommends a similar approach to colorectal cancer surveillance due to the lack of SBA-specific data [SBA-6, SBA-7]. The schedule is as follows: history and physical examination every 3-6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years; CEA and CA 19-9 every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years; chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast every 6-12 months for 2 years, then every 12 months for years 3-5. FDG-PET/CT is not indicated for routine surveillance. Routine capsule endoscopy is not indicated [SBA-6, SBA-7]. Patients with Lynch syndrome should have appropriate screenings commensurate with their genotype and family cancer history, which may include small intestinal screening [SBA-A 2 of 2 reference 13]. Patients with Crohn's disease and history of SBA remain at elevated risk for further SBAs and surveillance screening should be considered [SBA-F].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: A retrospective study found that 39% of patients with SBA who underwent cancer-directed surgery developed recurrence, with median time to recurrence of 25 months [36]. Among recurrences, 57% were distant metastases, 19% carcinomatosis, 7% abdominal wall, and 17% local. Because of this high recurrence rate, routine surveillance is recommended.
