However, no prospective studies have validated surveillance intervals for SBA, so the Panel endorses the colorectal cancer surveillance paradigm [SBA-6 footnote p].
late_effects_screening: Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy: screen for persistent numbness, tingling, or pain. Only treat pain component with duloxetine, gabapentin, or pregabalin [SBA-F]
Chronic diarrhea or malabsorption: especially after Whipple surgery or extensive small bowel resection; manage with pancreatic enzymes, antidiarrheals, and dietary modifications
Secondary primary malignancies: patients with Lynch syndrome, FAP, or PJS are at risk for other cancers (colorectal, endometrial, gastric, etc.) and should undergo appropriate screening [SBA-1]
Crohn's disease patients: remain at risk for further SBAs; surveillance endoscopy may be considered [SBA-F]
Celiac disease patients: continue gluten-free diet; association with SBA suggests possible increased risk of metachronous lesions [SBA-F]
Psychosocial and functional status: screen for distress, fatigue, sexual dysfunction, and fertility concerns; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Recurrence after curative-intent treatment is common. Local recurrence is less frequent than distant metastases (17% local vs. 57% distant) [36]. Peritoneal carcinomatosis occurs in 25-50% of metastatic cases [108]. Distant metastases most commonly involve the liver and peritoneal cavity. Elevated CEA or CA 19-9 may precede radiographic recurrence. Workup for suspected recurrence includes chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast, endoscopy if indicated, and biomarker testing (including RAS, BRAF V600E, HER2, MMR/MSI, TMB, NTRK, RET, POLE/POLD1). FDG-PET/CT may be considered if CT/MR results are equivocal [SBA-8].

clinical_follow_up_schedule.components: History and physical examination, CEA, CA 19-9, chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 6-12 months

clinical_follow_up_schedule.frequency: Every 6 months for H&P and tumor markers; annually for CT
clinical_follow_up_schedule.components: History and physical examination every 6 months, CEA and CA 19-9 every 6 months, chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 12 months
Continue surveillance based on risk factors (e.g., Lynch syndrome, Crohn's disease). Consider small bowel endoscopy for high-risk patients.

imaging_strategy: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with IV iodinated contrast and oral contrast is the standard for surveillance. If IV contrast is contraindicated, MR abdomen/pelvis with gadolinium-based contrast may be used (specific agents: gadobutrol, gadopentetate dimeglumine, gadobenate dimeglumine, gadoteridol in chronic kidney disease) [SBA-A 1 of 2 reference 1]
CT enterography is not routinely indicated for surveillance but may be reserved for instances of clinical necessity when primary tumor is poorly visualized by standard methods [SBA-A 2 of 2]

FDG-PET/CT is not indicated for routine surveillance. It may be considered if CT or MR results are equivocal, including evaluation of potential peritoneal disease where lesions are larger than the lower limits of FDG-PET detection [SBA-A 1 of 2]
Capsule endoscopy is not indicated for routine surveillance and is contraindicated if small bowel obstruction or strictures exist [SBA-A 2 of 2]
laboratory_monitoring: CEA and CA 19-9 every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for a total of 5 years [SBA-6, SBA-7]
CBC and chemistry profile may be monitored to assess for therapy-related toxicities and overall health status
For patients on immunotherapy, monitor thyroid function, liver function, and other irAE labs per NCCN guidelines for immune-related toxicity management
For patients on targeted therapies (e.g., dabrafenib/trametinib, selpercatinib), follow prescribing information for required monitoring (e.g., LFTs, creatinine, cardiac function)
supportive_follow_up: Survivorship care planning: provide patient with a treatment summary including dates of surgery, chemotherapy (drugs, cycles), and radiation (dose, fields) [SBA-F]
Manage late/long-term sequelae: peripheral neuropathy, fatigue, pain, sexual dysfunction, distress. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship [SBA-F]
Lifestyle modifications: encourage plant-based diet, healthy body weight, regular physical activity, avoidance of alcohol and tobacco. See NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation [SBA-F]
Patients with confirmed celiac disease: avoid gluten [SBA-F]
Patients with Crohn's disease: remain at elevated risk for further SBAs; consider surveillance screening [SBA-F references 1,2]
Fertility counseling: discussed at end of treatment [SBA-F]
All age- and gender-appropriate preventive health and cancer screenings should be maintained with primary care provider [SBA-F]

special_situations.situation: Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer, HNPCC)
special_situations.key_message: All patients with SBA should be counseled for familial malignancies and considered for risk assessment of Lynch syndrome, FAP, PJS, and other polypoid mutations [SBA-1]. SBA has a higher percentage of MSI-H/dMMR tumors compared to colorectal cancer [34,35], making universal MMR/MSI testing important for identifying Lynch syndrome.
special_situations.management: Universal MMR or MSI testing is recommended in all newly diagnosed patients with SBA [SBA-B]. Patients with Lynch syndrome have an estimated lifetime SBA risk of 4% (relative risk >100) [27,28]. Affected individuals should undergo appropriate screenings commensurate with their genotype and family cancer history, which may include small intestinal screening [SBA-A 2 of 2 reference 13]. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric.

special_situations.key_message: Patients with FAP develop large numbers of adenomatous polyps; lifetime SBA risk estimated 3-5% [20]. The duodenum and periampullary region are the most common locations for SBA in FAP.
special_situations.management: Patients should be considered for risk assessment [SBA-1]. Endoscopic surveillance of the duodenum is recommended per NCCN Colorectal Cancer Screening guidelines. Polyps may be managed endoscopically if feasible; surgery (pancreaticoduodenectomy) for advanced disease.

special_situations.key_message: Autosomal dominant condition with hamartomatous/adenomatous polyps predominantly in jejunum and ileum. Relative risk of SBA is 520 compared to general population [25]; lifetime risk 1.7-13% [20,25,26].
special_situations.management: Patients should be counseled for familial malignancies and considered for risk assessment [SBA-1]. Surveillance with video capsule endoscopy or MR enteroclysis may be used to detect small bowel polyps [175,176,180]. Polypectomy via double-balloon enteroscopy for large polyps.

special_situations.key_message: Association with SBA is recognized, with 0.06% of celiac disease patients diagnosed with SBA within 1 year of diagnosis in a large cohort [17]. Consider studies for celiac disease in SBA workup [SBA-1].
special_situations.management: Lifelong gluten-free diet is essential. SBA risk persists; surveillance endoscopy may be considered. PD-L1 expression may be higher in SBA associated with celiac disease [123].

special_situations.key_message: Patients with Crohn's disease have increased risk of distal SBA. Relative risk of small bowel cancer estimated 3.70 (95% CI, 1.23-11.13) in a cohort of 9100 patients [11]. Patients with Crohn's disease and history of SBA remain at elevated risk for further SBAs [SBA-F].
special_situations.management: Surveillance screening should be considered for these individuals [SBA-F; references Grolleau 2017 [13], Cahill 2014 [12]]. Endoscopic screening for dysplasia may be used, though sensitivity is low (33% in one study) [174].

special_situations.key_message: Affects 25-50% of stage IV SBA patients, more common with jejunal/ileal primaries. Carries poor prognosis with median OS 5.9 months [109].
special_situations.management: For resectable peritoneal carcinomatosis, surgical cytoreduction may be considered with multidisciplinary team evaluation at experienced center. HIPEC cannot be recommended based on lack of evidence (PRODIGE 7 in CRC showed no benefit of HIPEC vs cytoreduction alone) [115]. Systemic therapy is the mainstay for unresectable disease.

special_situations.key_message: Potentially resectable visceral or peritoneal metastases are extremely rare for SBA [SBA-8]. Resection may be considered for select patients with limited metastases.
special_situations.management: Retrospective data: 5-year survival after liver resection for small bowel cancer metastases was 49% (median OS 58 months) [106]. Another series of 34 patients undergoing SBA metastasis resection reported median OS 28.2 months, relapse-free survival 18.7 months, and 44.1% survived >3 years [107]. Poor differentiation, invaded margins, and lymphatic invasion were poor prognostic factors. If metastasectomy is considered, a multidisciplinary team including an experienced surgeon should be consulted.

special_situations.key_message: Distinguish from duodenal SBA using tumor location: epicenter should not be at ampulla, and >75% of mass should not be within ampulla for duodenal primary. EUS may be helpful [SBA-A, SBA-B].
special_situations.management: Ampullary cancer is managed per NCCN Guidelines for Ampullary Adenocarcinoma [SBA-1].

special_situations.situation: dMMR/MSI-H or POLE/POLD1 ultra-hypermutated phenotype (TMB>50 mut/Mb)
special_situations.key_message: Enriched in SBA compared to CRC. Important for treatment selection: checkpoint inhibitors recommended in neoadjuvant, adjuvant (with chemotherapy), and metastatic settings. POLE/POLD1 mutations confer ultra-hypermutated phenotype and significant benefit with checkpoint inhibitors [152-154].
special_situations.management: Neoadjuvant: consider checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy for T4 or bulky primary [SBA-3, SBA-5]. Adjuvant: for T4, N0, M0 or T Any, N1-2, consider FOLFOX + atezolizumab or CAPEOX + atezolizumab (category 2A) [SBA-7]. Metastatic: checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1, PD-L1, PD-1/CTLA-4) are recommended for any line of therapy [SBA-D 2 of 12].
special_situations.evidence: Garmezy 2022 [153]; Mosalem 2024 [152]; Schrock 2017 [35]; KEYNOTE-158 [142]; ZEBRA [143]; GARNET [149,150]

special_situations.situation: Elderly patients or those not candidates for intensive therapy
special_situations.key_message: For patients who are older, complete geriatric assessment to aid appropriate prediction of treatment risks. See NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology [SBA-D 3 footnote h].
special_situations.management: Initial therapy options include capecitabine ± bevacizumab or fluorouracil/leucovorin ± bevacizumab [SBA-D 1 of 12]. Checkpoint inhibitors are also options for dMMR/MSI-H tumors regardless of age.

clinical_pearls: Universal MMR/MSI testing is recommended for all newly diagnosed SBA patients, as dMMR/MSI-H is more common in SBA than CRC (up to 10-15%), has prognostic significance, and guides immunotherapy use [SBA-B].

For resectable SBA, adequate lymph node dissection (≥8 nodes) is critical for accurate staging and is associated with improved survival; <5 nodes for duodenal or <8 for jejunal/ileal primaries is considered high-risk [SBA-C, SBA-B].

Adjuvant therapy for SBA is largely extrapolated from colorectal cancer data; the BALLAD trial (phase III) is ongoing to address this question. For stage III pMMR/MSS, FOLFOX or CAPEOX for 3-6 months is recommended; for stage II with high-risk features (T4, positive margins, inadequate LN harvest, perforation), consider similar therapy [SBA-6].

For metastatic disease, biomarker testing (RAS, BRAF V600E, HER2, MMR/MSI, TMB, NTRK, RET, POLE/POLD1) is essential to identify actionable targets and guide therapy selection. TMB-high (>10 mut/Mb) is seen in 9-11% of SBA cases [SBA-8].

Checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, certain PD-1/PD-L1 agents) are recommended for dMMR/MSI-H advanced SBA for any line of therapy, with some agents included based on class effect from other tumor types [SBA-D 2 of 12].

Oxaliplatin neurotoxicity: discontinue oxaliplatin after 3-4 months (or sooner for unacceptable toxicity) while maintaining other agents; duloxetine/gabapentin only for pain component, not for numbness/tingling [SBA-D 3, SBA-F].

Radiation therapy is primarily reserved for duodenal primaries with positive margins after resection (sequential chemo/RT) or for locally unresectable disease as conversion strategy. For jejunal/ileal lesions, RT is generally not indicated [SBA-E].

Patients with Lynch syndrome, FAP, PJS, celiac disease, or Crohn's disease require genetic counseling and tailored surveillance due to elevated risk of metachronous SBA and other malignancies [SBA-1, SBA-F].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma (Version 2.2026)
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive management recommendations including diagnosis, staging, treatment of localized and metastatic disease, surveillance, and survivorship. Includes principles of imaging, pathology, surgery, systemic therapy, and radiation therapy. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017) – Small Intestine Adenocarcinoma
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines TNM staging for SBA: T1-4, N0-2, M0-1. Staging groups: stage I (T1-2,N0), IIA (T3,N0), IIB (T4,N0), IIIA (any T,N1), IIIB (any T,N2), IV (M1) [ST-1].

guidelines_resources.organization: French Intergroup (SNFGE, FFCD, GERCOR, UNICANCER, SFCD, SFED, SFRO)
guidelines_resources.title: Small bowel adenocarcinoma: French intergroup clinical practice guidelines for diagnosis, treatments and follow-up
guidelines_resources.notes: First clinical practice guidelines for SBA (published 2018). Provides recommendations for management based on literature review and expert consensus [5].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Many SBA recommendations are extrapolated from colorectal cancer data, including DPYD testing, surveillance intervals, and certain systemic therapy regimens. Refer to this guideline for detailed information on biomarker testing and survivorship.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Colorectal, Endometrial, and Gastric
guidelines_resources.notes: Guidance for assessing hereditary cancer syndromes including Lynch syndrome, FAP, and PJS. Should be consulted for patients with SBA to evaluate familial risk [SBA-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for psychosocial support and distress screening in SBA patients [SBA-1].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides screening, evaluation, and treatment recommendations for long-term consequences of cancer treatment, including neuropathy, fatigue, pain, and sexual dysfunction [SBA-F].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for lifestyle modifications in survivorship [SBA-F].

key_trials.full_name: GLOBAL BALLAD: An International Rare Cancers Initiative trial to evaluate the potential benefit of adjuvant chemotherapy for small bowel adenocarcinoma (IRCI 002)
key_trials.population: Patients with resected stage I-III SBA
key_trials.key_result: Trial not yet completed; currently enrolling.
key_trials.practice_change: Pending results. Currently, adjuvant therapy for SBA is based on retrospective data and extrapolation from colon cancer.

key_trials.full_name: A Multicenter Phase II Study of Pembrolizumab in Patients with Advanced Small-Bowel Adenocarcinoma
key_trials.population: Previously treated advanced SBA (any MMR/MSI status)
key_trials.key_result: ORR did not meet prespecified endpoint; 50% of MSI-H patients (n=4) had partial response and remained alive without progression; one MSS patient had partial response (also TMB-H). Disease control rate 38%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: 63% grade ≥3 AEs, 28% grade 4-5 AEs. Median PFS 2.9 months overall.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports use of pembrolizumab in MSI-H advanced SBA; suggests TMB-H may also predict benefit.

key_trials.full_name: Efficacy of Pembrolizumab in Patients With Noncolorectal High Microsatellite Instability/Mismatch Repair-Deficient Cancer
key_trials.population: dMMR/MSI-H advanced non-CRC solid tumors, including SBA
key_trials.key_result: Whole population ORR 34.3%; SBA cohort ORR 42.1% (3 complete responses). Median PFS for SBA: 9.2 months. Median OS for SBA: not reached at publication.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Median duration of response not reached for SBA.
key_trials.practice_change: Major support for pembrolizumab in MSI-H advanced SBA. Led to FDA tissue-agnostic approval for dMMR/MSI-H tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab in patients with metastatic DNA mismatch repair-deficient or microsatellite instability-high colorectal cancer
key_trials.journal: Lancet Oncology (2017), Journal of Clinical Oncology (2018)
key_trials.population: dMMR/MSI-H metastatic colorectal cancer (no SBA specifically, but extrapolated)
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 2 weeks OR nivolumab + ipilimumab (3 mg/kg + 1 mg/kg) every 3 weeks x4 then nivolumab maintenance
key_trials.key_result: Nivolumab monotherapy: ORR 31.1% (95% CI 20.8-42.9), 1-year PFS 50%, 1-year OS 73%. Nivolumab + ipilimumab: ORR 55% (95% CI 45.2-63.8), 1-year PFS 71%, 1-year OS 85%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3-4 AEs: 20% monotherapy, 32% combination. Significant improvements in patient-reported outcomes in combination arm.
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab ± ipilimumab as standard in dMMR/MSI-H CRC; extended to SBA by class effect.

key_trials.full_name: A phase II basket trial of dual anti-CTLA-4 and anti-PD-1 blockade in rare tumors: Small bowel cancer cohort
key_trials.population: Rare tumors including small bowel cancer
key_trials.key_result: ORR 8% (1 complete response, 1 partial response). Median PFS 2 months, median OS 6 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Toxicity profile consistent with known irAEs.
key_trials.practice_change: Modest activity in unselected small bowel cancer; supports biomarker-directed use (MSI-H).

key_trials.full_name: Phase 2 ROAR trial: Dabrafenib plus trametinib in BRAF V600E-mutated rare cancers
key_trials.n: 3 patients with adenocarcinoma of small intestine (in cohort, total n across cohorts 181)
key_trials.population: BRAF V600E-mutated advanced rare cancers, including SBA
key_trials.intervention: Dabrafenib 150 mg BID + trametinib 2 mg daily
key_trials.key_result: ORR 67% (2 of 3) in small intestine cohort. For all cohorts: median DoR 7.7 months in small intestine, median PFS not evaluable, median OS 21.8 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 AEs in 87.9% across all cohorts; pyrexia most common (40.8%). Fatal SAEs in 4.4%.
key_trials.practice_change: FDA approval of dabrafenib + trametinib for BRAF V600E-mutated solid tumors (tissue-agnostic) [161].

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Patients With HER2-Expressing Solid Tumors
key_trials.n: 267 (1 patient with intestinal adenocarcinoma in rare tumor cohort)"
key_trials.key_result: Overall ORR 37.1% (95% CI 31.3-43.2). In IHC 3+ patients (n=75): ORR 61.3%, median DOR 22.1 mo, median PFS 11.9 mo, median OS 21.1 mo. ILD/pneumonitis in 20.5%.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports use of fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2 IHC 3+ solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (T-DXd) in Patients With HER2-Overexpressing/Amplified Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
key_trials.population: HER2-overexpressing/amplified (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+) mCRC (extrapolated to SBA)
key_trials.intervention: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan 5.4 mg/kg or 6.4 mg/kg every 3 weeks
key_trials.practice_change: Supports 5.4 mg/kg dosing; FDA approval in HER2-positive CRC extended to all solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Repotrectinib in Patients with NTRK Fusion-Positive Advanced Solid Tumors
key_trials.population: NTRK fusion-positive advanced solid tumors (including 3 CRC patients, 0 SBA)
key_trials.intervention: Repotrectinib 160 mg daily for 14 days then 160 mg BID
key_trials.key_result: TKI-naïve: ORR 58%, 12-mo DOR 86%, 12-mo PFS 56%. TKI-pretreated: ORR 50%, 12-mo DOR 39%, 12-mo PFS 22%. Grade ≥3 AEs 51%.
key_trials.practice_change: Repotrectinib approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Selpercatinib in Patients with RET Fusion-Positive Solid Tumors
key_trials.population: RET fusion-positive solid tumors (non-lung/non-thyroid), including SBA
key_trials.intervention: Selpercatinib 160 mg BID (≥50 kg) or 120 mg BID (<50 kg)
key_trials.key_result: Overall ORR 43.9% (95% CI 28.5-60.3). For SBA patient: complete response, DOR 24.5 months. Median PFS 13.2 months, median OS 18 months.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Common grade ≥3 AEs: hypertension, transaminitis. Treatment-related SAEs: drug-induced liver injury, fatigue, hypersensitivity.
key_trials.practice_change: FDA tissue-agnostic approval for RET fusion-positive solid tumors [172].

key_trials.full_name: Dostarlimab in Patients with Mismatch Repair-Deficient Solid Cancers
key_trials.population: dMMR/MSI-H advanced solid tumors, including SBA (n=23) and POLE-mutated (n=11)
key_trials.intervention: Dostarlimab-gxly 500 mg IV every 3 weeks x4 then 1000 mg IV every 6 weeks
key_trials.key_result: For small-intestinal cohort: ORR 39.1% (95% CI 19.7-61.5), 21.7% complete response rate. For overall efficacy population: median DOR not reached, median PFS 8.1 mo, median OS 31.6 mo. POLE-mutated (n=11): ORR 54.5%, PFS ≥1 year.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Immune-related AEs: hypothyroidism 6.9%, ALT increase 5.8%, arthralgia 4.7%.
key_trials.practice_change: FDA approval of dostarlimab for dMMR advanced solid tumors [148].

key_trials.full_name: Phase II Study of Capecitabine and Oxaliplatin for Advanced Adenocarcinoma of the Small Bowel and Ampulla of Vater
key_trials.population: Advanced SBA and ampullary adenocarcinoma, first-line
key_trials.intervention: CAPEOX (capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14, oxaliplatin 130 mg/m2 day 1 every 21 days)
key_trials.key_result: ORR 50% (including 10% complete response). Median PFS and OS not reported for whole cohort, but median PFS 5.9 months and OS 17.3 months from another phase II (Horimatsu 2017) [128].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Common AEs: neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, nausea, diarrhea, peripheral neuropathy, fatigue.
key_trials.practice_change: Established CAPEOX as a standard first-line option for metastatic SBA.

key_trials.full_name: Second-line Chemotherapy with Fluorouracil, Leucovorin, and Irinotecan (FOLFIRI regimen) in Patients with Advanced Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma After Failure of First-Line Platinum-Based Chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Advanced SBA after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: ORR 20%, disease control rate 52%. Median PFS 3.2 months, median OS 10.5 months. Grade 3-4 toxicity in 48%.
key_trials.practice_change: Supports FOLFIRI as second-line therapy after oxaliplatin failure in metastatic SBA.
